query: evidence on improving reading comprehension in adults with mild intellectual disability

retrieved_docs.title: Learning Disability -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: As with other neurodevelopmental conditions, monitoring developmental progression and inquiring about the child’s academic performance and school behavior is important. For those with associated behavioral problems, appropriate treatment should also be considered. Furthermore, several remedial programs work with children with reading and writing difficulties. [16] Specific educational strategies in management: In children with dyslexia (decoding problems), phonological awareness needs to be increased, which includes the ability to manipulate and hear individual phonemes (the sound structure of words), such as 'k' in kite or 'b' in bat. Besides phonemic awareness, letter-sound proficiency is remediated. The repeated practice of oral reading may aid in improving fluency. Children with dysgraphia can benefit from hand-eye exercises that improve coordination. Educational strategies addressing dyscalculia include practicing number syntax

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Progressive Aphasia -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Primary preventive measures for PPA focus on reducing the risks associated with neurodegenerative diseases and promoting brain health. These measures include maintaining a healthy lifestyle with a proper diet, regular exercise, and mental stimulation through activities like reading and puzzle-solving. Managing cardiovascular risk factors like hypertension, diabetes, and obesity is crucial, as cardiovascular health is closely linked to brain health. Additionally, protecting against head injuries and maintaining social connections can help preserve cognitive function and reduce the risk of cognitive decline.

retrieved_docs.title: Intellectual Disability -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The cognitive deficit of intellectual disability is permanent damage; thus, the prognosis of intellectual disability depends on the severity of cognitive impairment and the supportive environment provided. If provided adequate support, individuals with mild intellectual disabilities may achieve adequate language and social competency to live independently and raise their own families. Individuals with moderate intellectual disabilities are not likely to achieve academic levels greater than second grade; however, they may acquire language and communicate their needs. They may be able to get a job under supervision. Individuals with severe intellectual disabilities usually have difficulty acquiring language. They may be able to develop alternative communication skills and recognize words that are critical to functioning. Adults with severe intellectual disability usually require supervised living situations, such as group homes, and supervision to perform any work-related tasks. Individuals with profound intellectual disabilities have significant difficulty with communication and social functioning.

retrieved_docs.title: Intellectual Disability -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Educational support is a crucial component of intellectual disability management. Upon diagnosis of intellectual disability in children, healthcare providers must contact the school promptly to set up special education arrangements. What special education entails may vary slightly among schools, but it typically aids comprehensively with providing academic modifications as well as transition planning from childhood to adulthood with a focus on promoting self-sufficiency. It also teaches them how to seek assistance, behavioral skills, vocational skills, communication skills, functional living skills, and social skills based on individual needs in the least restrictive environment. Creating the least restrictive environment for students with an intellectual disability means placing them in general classrooms as much as tolerated. While segregated classrooms for special needs may be helpful for those who need them, implementing the least restrictive environment policy has been shown to improve student outcomes. Monitoring the patients' progress in school is important because education solely from classroom settings may not be sufficient. If an individual with an intellectual disability requires assistance beyond what is available at school, the family can meet the need at home through family education or other outside resources. For example, a disabled student who needs extra attention with social skills may be referred to participate in the Special Olympics programs that have been shown to improve social competence in intellectual disability subjects. Lastly, other causes of learning difficulties need to be ruled out, and appropriate hearing and vision screenings are necessary for individuals with intellectual disabilities.

retrieved_docs.title: Dyslexia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: As a clinician, it is crucial to work with parents, schools, and caregivers to identify patients with dyslexia early. However, most treatment is provided at school and at home. Early intervention will focus on vocabulary, comprehension, and necessary skills such as phonics. As students progress through their education, the intervention will move to improve understanding of word meanings, improved comprehension, more challenging reading, and strategies to excel. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Learning Disability -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Learning disabilities refer to several disorders that may affect the acquisition, organization, retention, comprehension, or the use of verbal or nonverbal information. This activity outlines the roles of primary care pediatricians, developmental specialists, and other interprofessional team members in assessing, managing, and optimizing outcomes for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Dyslexia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Reading is a skill unique to humans and is crucial for living in the world today. [1] Learning to read is one of the first teaching points of elementary school. Children are many times cast into expected long-term education success tiers based on their ability to learn to read. Dyslexia (developmental dyslexia) is when children have a difficult time learning to decode words. Decoding is the accuracy or fluency one develops when reading aloud. [2] These children struggle with sounding out words and recognizing “sight words.” They have poor reading fluency and a difficult time with spelling. [2] These children typically do not struggle with oral or nonverbal abilities. [1] It is important to note that the reading disability cannot be accounted for by decreased educational opportunities or low intellectual capacity. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Special Education -- Issues of Concern -- Standard Terms Used in Special Education
retrieved_docs.content: Remediation: Interventions are given to help the student who has fallen behind academically to catch up. The student may or may not have special needs, or their special needs may not have been identified.

retrieved_docs.title: Learning Disability -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Learning disabilities are a type of neurodevelopmental disorder that impedes normal ability to learn and perform academically Learning Disorders are specified into 3 major academic domains 1. Reading 2. Writing 3. Mathematics Criterion A: Difficulties processing instruction and applying skills as demonstrated by at least 1 of the following symptoms for at least 6 months disturbance of reading disturbance of comprehension disturbance of spelling disturbance of written expression disturbance of number sense disturbance of mathematical reasoning Criterion B: Academic skill is substantially lower than expected when compared to standard, resulting in significant dysfunction Criterion C: Difficulties onset during school-age years Criterion D: Aberrations are not better explained by any neurological, developmental, motor, or sensory (hearing or vision) disorder. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Learning Disability -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In addition, comprehensive neuropsychological testing can further guide the clinician toward the correct diagnosis. Commonly administered tests include: Woodcock-Johnson-III and Wide Range Achievement Test to evaluate academic achievement; the Adaptive Behavior Assessment System-II and the Vineland Adaptive Behavior Scale-II to assess adaptive behavior; the Conners Rating Scale, ADHD Rating Scale-IV (formerly DuPaul scale) and NICHQ Vanderbilt Parent and Teacher Assessment Scales to evaluate for attention and hyperactive disorders; the Wechsler Intelligence Scales for Children—Fourth Edition (WISC-IV) for evaluation of general cognition; the Achenbach Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) for assessment of general behavior; and the Clinical Evaluation of Language Fundamentals (4th Ed.)

retrieved_docs.title: Aphasia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Patients may experience difficulty articulating words, forming sentences, deficits in comprehension, or a combination of these. Symptoms vary from mild word-finding issues to severe language deficits. Evaluation includes neuroimaging and standardized assessments, such as the Boston Diagnostic Aphasia Examination and the Western Aphasia Battery. Treatment addresses the underlying cause and involves a multidisciplinary approach, including speech therapy and support for physical and mental health comorbidities. This activity highlights the evaluation and treatment of aphasia and the essential role of the interprofessional healthcare team in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Cortical Atrophy -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: It is of paramount importance that patient and their families are informed about the diagnosis and accompanying functional disabilities. Patients should be encouraged to reduce clutter around the house, use non-visual cues and arrange for twenty-four-hour supervision when required. Assistance with potentially dangerous and demanding tasks like cooking and formal driving evaluations by specialized therapists will help determine and ensure safety. Patients will likely benefit by working with occupational therapists and adopting compensation strategies tailored to individual deficits.

retrieved_docs.title: Special Education -- Issues of Concern -- Standard Terms Used in Special Education
retrieved_docs.content: Related services: Interventions that are not strictly educational but help the student benefit from the overall academic support they receive. They may include counseling, occupational therapy, physical therapy, speech and language therapy, and transportation.

retrieved_docs.title: Dyslexia -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: High achieving and high IQ students may be overlooked early in their educational careers. They can still have dyslexia but compensate in other ways and miss their window for early intervention. This can have a drastic effect on their outlook towards schooling as reading becomes more complicated and have a lasting impact on their ability to learn. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790
retrieved_docs.content: Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome is characterized by an impairment in interpretation, a deficit that extends to both neurobehavioral and systemic domains. This impaired interpretive ability is specifically linked to اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي), a practical form of social communication disorder, indicating that individuals with the syndrome may experience difficulties in processing and responding to social cues in everyday interactions. In addition, the same interpretive impairment affects the understanding of قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic kidney failure), suggesting that the syndrome can also hinder accurate perception and management of renal disease-related information. Together, these relationships highlight how Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome disrupts cognitive interpretation, leading to challenges in social communication and the comprehension of chronic kidney pathology, which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2473
retrieved_docs.content: Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome is associated with a marked impairment in interpretation functions. This impaired interpretation of information contributes to cognitive disturbances that can present as dementia, senile decline, and acute confused states (verwardheidstoestand). In addition, the syndrome’s effect on interpretive abilities extends to the social domain, manifesting as a practical social communication disorder (اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي)). Recognizing that Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome leads to impaired interpretation of both cognitive and social cues highlights the need for comprehensive assessment of memory, orientation, and communicative competence in patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2687
retrieved_docs.content: Impaired is a descriptive term that can denote functional deficits in distinct clinical contexts. In the neurodevelopmental domain, Impaired is interpreted as اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي), a practical social communication disorder that reflects difficulties in interacting socially. In the renal domain, Impaired is also used as an interpretation of قصور كلوي مزمن, indicating chronic kidney disease and the progressive loss of kidney function. The renal condition chronic kidney disease (قصور كلوي مزمن) is clinically associated with التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis), underscoring that glomerular inflammation often contributes to or co‑exists with long‑term renal impairment. Recognizing that the label “Impaired” may refer to either social communication deficits or chronic kidney dysfunction, and that glomerulonephritis is linked to chronic kidney disease, helps clinicians accurately interpret the term in context and tailor patient assessment and management accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2932
retrieved_docs.content: Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome is characterized by an impairment in the ability to interpret information, and this impairment is understood as a practical social communication disorder (اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي)).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome is linked to an impaired ability to interpret information, as reflected by the concept of “interpretation_of.” This impairment is manifested in several clinical contexts: it is associated with dementia, senile type with an acute confused state, it is interpreted as اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي) (social communication disorder, practical), and it also relates to قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic kidney failure). The syndrome’s effect on interpretation therefore connects neurocognitive decline, acute confusion, functional communication deficits, and renal insufficiency, underscoring the importance of integrated evaluation and management of these interrelated conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2628
retrieved_docs.content: Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome is linked to an impairment in interpretation, a deficit that is expressed as اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي) (practical social‑communication disorder) and also interferes with the interpretation of قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic kidney failure). Chronic kidney failure itself is clinically associated with التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis). Consequently, the syndrome’s impact on interpretive abilities may affect both social communication and the clinical understanding of renal pathology, highlighting the importance of addressing interpretive deficits in patients who present with these overlapping neurological and nephrological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 254
retrieved_docs.content: integrating various organic‑based mood and transient disturbances that do not fit more specific categories. This structured framework aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 541
retrieved_docs.content: Cystic fibrosis without meconium ileus is recognized as a specific form of cystic fibrosis and its clinical presentation is noted to have an impaired interpretation, indicating that the disease may be difficult to assess or classify. Similarly, Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome is described as having an impaired interpretation, suggesting challenges in understanding its manifestations. The term “Impaired” itself is linked to several distinct interpretations: it can denote dementia or senile cognitive decline accompanied by an acute confused state, it can represent a practical social communication disorder (اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي)), and it can be interpreted as chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). Chronic kidney failure is clinically associated with glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى), underscoring a frequent relationship between renal functional impairment and inflammatory kidney disease. Together, these connections illustrate how impaired functional or cognitive status can be a common thread across diverse conditions—from genetic respiratory disorders and rare neuro‑developmental syndromes to renal pathology—highlighting the importance of careful interpretation in diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1883
retrieved_docs.content: Impaired is used as an interpretive label for distinct clinical conditions across languages. In the Dutch context, the term Impaired functions as an interpretation_of “dementie; seniel, met acute verwardheidstoestand,” describing a senile dementia accompanied by an acute confused state. In the Arabic context, Impaired has_interpretation of “اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي),” which denotes a practical social communication disorder. Thus, the concept of Impaired bridges cognitive impairment observed in acute senile dementia and functional impairment seen in social communication difficulties, highlighting its role as a descriptive term for varied forms of mental and communicative dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2574
retrieved_docs.content: Emotional blunting (تبلد المشاعر) is identified as a clinical manifestation of specific brain atrophy (ضمور المخ المحدد). This relationship indicates that patients who exhibit localized cerebral atrophy may present with reduced emotional responsiveness, and recognizing emotional blunting as a sign of such atrophy can aid clinicians in diagnosing underlying neurological conditions where affective changes are a prominent feature.

retrieved_docs.content: Disorders usually diagnosed in infancy are understood to have a broader relationship with ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة, which denotes an unspecified childhood psychosis that is presently active.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1162
retrieved_docs.content: The syndrome “الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)” is characterized as a collection of related neuropsychiatric manifestations. It includes “اضطراب التصرف” as one of its constituent features, and “اللامبالاة” is also identified as a member of this syndrome. In addition, the SMQ encompasses deficits in attention (“خلل في الانتباه”), reduced ability to maintain focus (“ضعف التركيز”), and an early presentation of excessive daytime sleepiness (“فرط النوم في النهار الأولي”). Together, these elements define the clinical profile of “الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)”, indicating that patients with this condition may exhibit behavior disorder, apathy, attention and concentration problems, and initial excessive daytime sleepiness as interrelated components of the disorder.

Together, these interconnections emphasize the centrality of the brain in diverse pathological, diagnostic, and therapeutic contexts, and they underscore the importance of recognizing overlapping disease categories for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.
